item.title: Best pink PC cases in 2024
item.itunes:title: Best pink PC cases in 2024
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Building your PC means customizing it to suit your preferences. However, these days, having a unique build is such a challenging enterprise, thanks to most PC cases sharing similar designs. To overcome this problem and make their rigs unique, most people choose colorful cases instead of the default white and black options.

Pink cases are rather distinct. There aren't many on the market, so you'd be hard-pressed to find many rigs using them. However, we've scoured hundreds of pages to help you find the best pink PC cases to house your GPU, CPU, fans, and other components. While many brands below aren't mainstream manufacturers like NZXT and Corsair, they offer reliable cases with excellent radiator support, a roomy chassis, multiple expansion slots, and more.

  • Source: Amazon
    Vetroo AL600
    Best overall pink PC case
    $80 at Amazon
  • Source: Amazon
    Apevia Crusader-F-PK PC case
    Alternate best overall pink PC case
    $77 at Amazon
  • Apevia Prodigy-PK micro-ATX gaming case
    Best affordable pink PC case
    $75 at Amazon
  • Lian Li Lancool 205 Mesh
    Best pink PC case for airflow
    $125 at Amazon
  • Thermaltake Tower 200
    Best pink liquid cooling PC case
    $130 at Amazon
  • Vetroo M05 micro-ATX computer case
    Best pink micro-ATX case
    $60 at Amazon
  • Source: Silverstone
    SilverStone SG13P PC case
    Alternate best pink mini-ITX case
    $64 at Amazon
  • Source: Amazon
    Apevia Genesis Pro-G-PK PC case
    Best pink PC case with RGB
    $89 at Amazon

The top pink PC cases in 2024

Vetroo AL600
Best overall pink PC case

A great case that does everything pretty well

The Vetroo AL600 is a mid-tower ATX PC case that comes with a lot of great features for the price. It's available in pink, black, and white color options.

Pros
  • Six included RGB fans
  • Mesh front panel for better airflow
  • Tempered glass side panel
Cons
  • Half of the bottom fan is covered up
$80 at Amazon $116 at Newegg

The Vetroo AL600 is a mid-tower ATX gaming PC case available in pink, black, and white color variants. This PC case looks very similar to the Corsair 4000D Airflow PC case but is cheaper. The Vetroo AL600 case comes with a tempered glass side panel that lets you see the internals of the build. It uses a plain panel for the rest of the sides with vents for airflow. The front of the case has a mesh panel to offer maximum airflow and a dust filter to ensure that extra airflow doesn't mean more dust.

One of the best things about this PC case is that it has three pre-installed 120mm ARGB/PWM fans and a controller to control them. Two non-RGB fans are pre-installed on the PSU shroud and another at the back exhaust, bringing the AL600 to six total fans. The AL600 also offers pretty good radiator mounting options: 360mm at the front, 280mm at the top, and 120mm at the back.

The AL600 has seven horizontal expansion slots plus two vertical slots if you want to mount your GPU vertically. A separate compartment at the bottom holds the PSU and HDDs. You can also install more drives thanks to the dedicated mounting space at the back, behind the motherboard. There's enough space inside the chassis to even build a water-cooled PC, complete with a reservoir. Cable management should be pretty easy, thanks to the two wire combs installed behind the motherboard.

Overall, the Vetroo AL600 is a fantastic PC case that comes with all the essentials while only costing about $140. Many mainstream PC cases on the market with similar features cost significantly more than that. Most importantly, the AL600 doesn't exist just for being a pink PC case; it's a good PC case that's available in pink, an important distinction.

Apevia Crusader-F-PK PC case
Alternate best overall pink PC case

A good alternative if you want something for gamers

The Apevia Crusader-F-PK mid-tower PC case is a great option for those looking to build a gaming PC with a unique, striking design.

Pros
  • Four included RGB fans
  • Front panel is mesh
  • Relatively cheap
Cons
  • V-shaped front panel slightly covers the fans
  • No vertical GPU mounting
$77 at Amazon

If you're looking for something a little more edgy or gamer-y, I recommend checking out the ATX-sized Apevia Crusader-F-PK. The A-shaped front panel will probably catch your eye immediately, and although this technically means less intake airflow, realistically, it won't cause any noticeable temperature issues. The Crusader also has a glass side panel and RGB fans to complete the typical gaming desktop style with a twist of pink.

The Apevia Crusader-F-PK case has plenty of space to mount a bunch of case fans: three 120mm fans on the front, two 120mm fans on the top panel, and a single 120mm fan at the back. The good thing is that this case comes with four pre-installed 120mm fans -- three on the front and one on the back -- which is more than enough for most users. The Crusader can accommodate a 360mm radiator at the front, a 240mm one at the top, and a 120mm at the back. If you're more interested in air cooling, the Crusader's 165mm height limit should give you plenty of room.

The Crusader offers enough clearance for installing a GPU with a max length of up to 350mm. There are no vertical expansion slots, but you get seven PCIe expansion slots, which is enough to install more than one GPU plus other devices. There's also a separate compartment for installing the PSU at the bottom with more room to add HDDs. The Crusader also comes with dedicated drive mounts for SSDs and HDDs. The motherboard tray has cutouts for routing cables to the back, which will help you manage the internal cables properly to build a clean-looking PC with minimal cable clutter.

For about $80, this is one of the best pink-colored PC cases I could find on Amazon. You can also step up to the Vetroo AL600 by spending a little more money, but they're both equally good. The real difference comes down to whether or not you prefer minimalism or that classic, edgy, gamer-y style.

Apevia Prodigy-PK micro-ATX gaming case
Best affordable pink PC case

Affordable case with great layout

The Apevia Prodigy-PK case is an affordable option with excellent specs for a micro-ATX build. It has great airflow, RGB fans, top-notch layout, and more.

Pros
  • Relatively cheap
  • Comes with RGB fans
  • Has a good layout and airflow
Cons
  • Fans feel cheap
$75 at Amazon

The Apevia Prodigy-PK PC case is a great choice to work with if you're going for a budget build. It has a high-quality metal construction and a see-through tempered glass side panel, allowing you to show off your rig while keeping it safe. The exterior is a sleek, curvy design that depicts a sporty appearance and ensures the mesh on the front panel remains safe from damage.

The inside of the unit is spacious, as it's big enough to accommodate micro-ATX and mini-ITX motherboards. You can fit up to a 360mm GPU and up to 160mm coolers without affecting its two 2.5 and two 3.5-inch drive bays. On the front panel lies multiple I/O units, such as the three USB ports, audio ports, power button, RGB switch button, and more. The front panel also houses a dust filter that helps minimize dust intake and can be taken off for cleaning.

Unlike many other budget cases, the Apevia Prodigy has three pre-installed RGB fans. You can still enjoy proper airflow, even if you don't have the budget for an AIO cooler. Combine that with the full-sized mesh and large honeycomb design on the front panel, and you'll get nothing but excellent airflow from this case. While the fans feel cheap, they are quiet and can serve as a good substitute until you can get a proper cooler. Overall, this case offers good value for money and doesn't have too many drawbacks.

Lian Li Lancool 205 Mesh
Best pink PC case for airflow

Small mATX case with good heat dissipation

Lian Li's Lancool 205 Mesh is a high-quality mid-tower case with three included fans and a tempered glass side panel. It supports 280mm radiators at the front and top.

Pros
  • Great airflow mesh design
  • Has a unique bottom I/O
  • Tempered glass side panel
Cons
  • Compact ATX mid-tower
  • No front-facing dust filter
$125 at Amazon

Choosing a case with good airflow goes beyond finding one with multiple fans. There are many different cases with three fans that can't provide decent airflow, thanks to their front panel design. If you want to provide your PC with top-notch airflow, the Lian Li Lancool 205 Mesh C case is a great choice.

This case has three pre-installed A-RGB fans and thanks to its front-facing mesh panel, you can get efficient heat dissipation. Also, even with its small form factor, it can support up to five 120mm fans for a cool system. If you're going for a CPU liquid cooler, you can attach a 280mm radiator on the top or front panels. The fans will work efficiently as there will be no obstructions, providing the CPU and GPU coolers with cold air.

Thanks to its airflow-focused design, the Lian Li Lancool 205 Mesh C is actually one of the best cases for people who want good thermal performance despite its relatively compact size. Maximum liquid cooling performance isn't possible since you're limited to 280mm radiators, but that will only be a problem for users with CPUs with an extremely high power draw.

Thermaltake Tower 200
Best pink liquid cooling PC case

A unique case design in pink

Thermaltake's Tower 200 case series supports Mini-ITX motherboards and custom liquid cooling loops. If you're looking to build a unique PC, look no further than this stunning compact showcase chassis.

Pros
  • Great for airflow
  • Unique showcase design
  • Custom liquid cooling support
Cons
  • Compact dimensions
  • Pricey
$130 at Amazon

The Thermaltake Tower 200 is a showcase PC chassis, giving it a unique and radical appearance. While most cases adhere to the ATX standard, the Tower 200 has a unique design for aesthetic and practical purposes, mainly for easier building and maintenance and better airflow. Keep in mind that only Mini-ITX motherboards will fit inside this chassis.

You can install a 120mm or 140mm fan on the top, a 120mm or 140mm at the PSU cover, and two 120mm or 140mm fans on the right side. The PC case comes with two pre-installed fans, so you'll have to buy the rest separately. But if you're using an AIO liquid cooling kit, you can install a 280mm or 240mm radiator on the right panel. GPU clearance comes in at 380mm without the PSU cover, but you're looking at just 280mm with this bracket installed. Clearance for CPU air coolers is excellent and beats many other cases at 200mm.

Building and maintaining a computer inside this case is convenient, and there's plenty of easily accessible fresh air. Whether you're going with air cooling or liquid, the Tower 200 is a great choice.

Vetroo M05 micro-ATX computer case
Best pink micro-ATX case

Ideal for compact builds

The Vetroo M05 computer case is a mid-range PC case designed for micro ATX and ITX builds. It has ample space, one pre-installed fan, and space for three more fans.

Pros
  • Affordable
  • Tempered glass on both sides
  • Type-C ready
Cons
  • Has just one fan
$60 at Amazon

The Vetroo M05 is a good case if your goal is a compact build. While it offers nothing special, it's still a worthy upgrade from the M03. It has 4mm tempered glass on both sides, providing your components with top-notch protection. This case also sports a mesh front panel, removable magnetic dust filters on the top and bottom, and a mesh cover on the rear to help improve airflow and provide easy cleaning.

Just like the M03, the Vetroo M05 also has a single pre-installed fan. However, there's room for seven more 120mm fans, and it can handle a 240mm radiator with no issues. While the radiator size isn't anything special, the case has enough clearance for GPUs and air coolers at 340mm and 165mm, respectively. Thanks to this clearance, you can easily use the Vetroo M05 for mini-ITX builds, as you can fit large, high-end GPUs into the unit.

It has a simple I/O interface with a USB-C port, two USB-A ports, a reset button, and a power button. The inside is spacious, and cable management is great, too. While the lack of more fans makes it less of a deal, it's still a great case, especially if you already have fans or would be okay with purchasing more separately.

SilverStone SG13P PC case
Alternate best pink mini-ITX case

A budget mini-ITX case that comes in pink

The SilverStone SG13P is an affordable mini-ITX PC case that offers a lot of value for your money. You can consider this case for an entry to a mid-range PC build.

Pros
  • Truly small form factor
  • Enough room for two slot gaming GPUs
  • Cheap
Cons
  • Limited airflow
$64 at Amazon

The SilverStone SG13P is another great option to consider if you're leaning towards a mini-ITX PC build, especially if you want a more typical experience. Compared to the Vetroo M05, the SG13P has some trade-offs that make this chassis more suitable for a budget build than the high-end PC you can put inside the M05.

The SG13P is decently optimized for airflow, with vents on the front, side, and top panels. Intake airflow from the front is aided thanks to the front panel being mesh rather than solid. Unfortunately, that's all offset by limited fan and radiator support; you can only install a single 120mm or 140mm fan on the front and a 120mm or 140mm radiator for liquid cooling. The other vents don't have mounting holes for fans, though they are large enough to exhaust air freely.

Clearance for the GPU and CPU air coolers is also pretty limited. Graphics cards can only be 270mm or shorter and can't be thicker than two slots. CPU air cooler support is limited to 61mm, which is a strong incentive to use a 120mm or 140mm liquid cooler. This is all because the SG13P supports a standard ATX-sized PSU, which keeps the cost of building a PC in this chassis lower than other ITX cases.

SilverStone's SG13P is a great option for anyone trying to build an ITX PC on a budget, since cheaper parts generally consume less power and create less heat, making the lack of cooling potential in the SG13P a non-issue. Upgrading a PC designed around this chassis might be challenging, however.

Apevia Genesis Pro-G-PK PC case
Best pink PC case with RGB

You know what goes well with literally every color? Pink.

The Apevia Genesis Pro is a good option for those who like to have lots of RGB lights inside their PC case. This unit comes with six pre-installed RGB case fans.

Pros
  • Six included RGB fans
  • Tempered glass front and side panels
  • Relatively cheap
Cons
  • Front panel restricts airflow
$89 at Amazon

If you're somebody who likes to have an RGB light show inside your PC, then you might want to consider buying the Apevia Genesis G-PRO-PK. It comes with RGB fans and tempered glass panels for both the side and the front, allowing you to appreciate an obscene amount of color for under $100.

Although the Genesis is a bit pricier than some other cases on this list, you're getting what you pay for since this case comes with six preinstalled RGB fans. It's uncommon to find a case with that many fans included, let alone with RGB functionality. You will have to provide your own RGB controller, though.

It's good that the Genesis comes with all these fans because airflow isn't exactly optimal. The front panel is 100% tempered glass, which looks good but leaves only a little gap between the case and the panel for intake airflow. Even though you can install a 360mm radiator on the front and a 240mm on the top, the lack of fresh air will be a challenge for cooling. Thanks to its seven expansion slots, the Genesis supports graphics cards up to 350mm in length and any thickness. The clearance for the CPU air cooler is 160mm, which is enough for pretty much any model.

The Apevia Genesis Pro G-PRO-PK is first and foremost about appearances, even at the expense of practicality. At just under $100, it's actually a really good deal since it comes with six preinstalled RGB fans and glass panels on the front and side. Thermal performance might be a concern if you select high-end parts to pair with this case.

Best pink PC cases to buy: Final Thoughts

Pink cases might not be popular, but there are many good chassis you can get your hands on if you want a pink rig. While the ideal case will depend on the PC you intend to build, the options above are some of the best pink cases you can get. For instance, the Vetroo AL600 case is the best overall due to its relatively low price tag and excellent design.

Vetroo AL600
Best overall pink PC case

The Vetroo AL600 is a mid-tower ATX PC case that comes with a lot of great features for the price. It's available in pink, black, and white color options.

$80 at Amazon $116 at Newegg

At the same time, the Thermaltake AH T200 and Apevia Genesis Pro-G-PK provide top-notch functionality without compromising on looks.

While the above-mentioned brands may not be as popular as NZXT, Corsair, and the like, you're not skimping on quality or functionality. Once you've chosen the ideal case for your rig, you can go through our list of best CPUs and motherboards to determine the components to house in it. Also, remember to check out our XDA Computing Forums, where you can discuss your build, get product recommendations, and more.

Cons
  • V-shaped front panel slightly covers the fans
  • No vertical GPU mounting
$77 at Amazon

If you're looking for something a little more edgy or gamer-y, I recommend checking out the ATX-sized Apevia Crusader-F-PK. The A-shaped front panel will probably catch your eye immediately, and although this technically means less intake airflow, realistically, it won't cause any noticeable temperature issues. The Crusader also has a glass side panel and RGB fans to complete the typical gaming desktop style with a twist of pink.

The Apevia Crusader-F-PK case has plenty of space to mount a bunch of case fans: three 120mm fans on the front, two 120mm fans on the top panel, and a single 120mm fan at the back. The good thing is that this case comes with four pre-installed 120mm fans -- three on the front and one on the back -- which is more than enough for most users. The Crusader can accommodate a 360mm radiator at the front, a 240mm one at the top, and a 120mm at the back. If you're more interested in air cooling, the Crusader's 165mm height limit should give you plenty of room.

The Crusader offers enough clearance for installing a GPU with a max length of up to 350mm. There are no vertical expansion slots, but you get seven PCIe expansion slots, which is enough to install more than one GPU plus other devices. There's also a separate compartment for installing the PSU at the bottom with more room to add HDDs. The Crusader also comes with dedicated drive mounts for SSDs and HDDs. The motherboard tray has cutouts for routing cables to the back, which will help you manage the internal cables properly to build a clean-looking PC with minimal cable clutter.

For about $80, this is one of the best pink-colored PC cases I could find on Amazon. You can also step up to the Vetroo AL600 by spending a little more money, but they're both equally good. The real difference comes down to whether or not you prefer minimalism or that classic, edgy, gamer-y style.

Apevia Prodigy-PK micro-ATX gaming case
Best affordable pink PC case

Affordable case with great layout

The Apevia Prodigy-PK case is an affordable option with excellent specs for a micro-ATX build. It has great airflow, RGB fans, top-notch layout, and more.

Pros
  • Relatively cheap
  • Comes with RGB fans
  • Has a good layout and airflow
Cons
  • Fans feel cheap
$75 at Amazon

The Apevia Prodigy-PK PC case is a great choice to work with if you're going for a budget build. It has a high-quality metal construction and a see-through tempered glass side panel, allowing you to show off your rig while keeping it safe. The exterior is a sleek, curvy design that depicts a sporty appearance and ensures the mesh on the front panel remains safe from damage.

The inside of the unit is spacious, as it's big enough to accommodate micro-ATX and mini-ITX motherboards. You can fit up to a 360mm GPU and up to 160mm coolers without affecting its two 2.5 and two 3.5-inch drive bays. On the front panel lies multiple I/O units, such as the three USB ports, audio ports, power button, RGB switch button, and more. The front panel also houses a dust filter that helps minimize dust intake and can be taken off for cleaning.

Unlike many other budget cases, the Apevia Prodigy has three pre-installed RGB fans. You can still enjoy proper airflow, even if you don't have the budget for an AIO cooler. Combine that with the full-sized mesh and large honeycomb design on the front panel, and you'll get nothing but excellent airflow from this case. While the fans feel cheap, they are quiet and can serve as a good substitute until you can get a proper cooler. Overall, this case offers good value for money and doesn't have too many drawbacks.

Lian Li Lancool 205 Mesh
Best pink PC case for airflow

Small mATX case with good heat dissipation

Lian Li's Lancool 205 Mesh is a high-quality mid-tower case with three included fans and a tempered glass side panel. It supports 280mm radiators at the front and top.

Pros
  • Great airflow mesh design
  • Has a unique bottom I/O
  • Tempered glass side panel
Cons
  • Compact ATX mid-tower
  • No front-facing dust filter
$125 at Amazon

Choosing a case with good airflow goes beyond finding one with multiple fans. There are many different cases with three fans that can't provide decent airflow, thanks to their front panel design. If you want to provide your PC with top-notch airflow, the Lian Li Lancool 205 Mesh C case is a great choice.

This case has three pre-installed A-RGB fans and thanks to its front-facing mesh panel, you can get efficient heat dissipation. Also, even with its small form factor, it can support up to five 120mm fans for a cool system. If you're going for a CPU liquid cooler, you can attach a 280mm radiator on the top or front panels. The fans will work efficiently as there will be no obstructions, providing the CPU and GPU coolers with cold air.

Thanks to its airflow-focused design, the Lian Li Lancool 205 Mesh C is actually one of the best cases for people who want good thermal performance despite its relatively compact size. Maximum liquid cooling performance isn't possible since you're limited to 280mm radiators, but that will only be a problem for users with CPUs with an extremely high power draw.

Thermaltake Tower 200
Best pink liquid cooling PC case

A unique case design in pink

Thermaltake's Tower 200 case series supports Mini-ITX motherboards and custom liquid cooling loops. If you're looking to build a unique PC, look no further than this stunning compact showcase chassis.

Pros
  • Great for airflow
  • Unique showcase design
  • Custom liquid cooling support
Cons
  • Compact dimensions
  • Pricey
$130 at Amazon

The Thermaltake Tower 200 is a showcase PC chassis, giving it a unique and radical appearance. While most cases adhere to the ATX standard, the Tower 200 has a unique design for aesthetic and practical purposes, mainly for easier building and maintenance and better airflow. Keep in mind that only Mini-ITX motherboards will fit inside this chassis.

You can install a 120mm or 140mm fan on the top, a 120mm or 140mm at the PSU cover, and two 120mm or 140mm fans on the right side. The PC case comes with two pre-installed fans, so you'll have to buy the rest separately. But if you're using an AIO liquid cooling kit, you can install a 280mm or 240mm radiator on the right panel. GPU clearance comes in at 380mm without the PSU cover, but you're looking at just 280mm with this bracket installed. Clearance for CPU air coolers is excellent and beats many other cases at 200mm.

Building and maintaining a computer inside this case is convenient, and there's plenty of easily accessible fresh air. Whether you're going with air cooling or liquid, the Tower 200 is a great choice.

Vetroo M05 micro-ATX computer case
Best pink micro-ATX case

Ideal for compact builds

The Vetroo M05 computer case is a mid-range PC case designed for micro ATX and ITX builds. It has ample space, one pre-installed fan, and space for three more fans.

Pros
  • Affordable
  • Tempered glass on both sides
  • Type-C ready
Cons
  • Has just one fan
$60 at Amazon

The Vetroo M05 is a good case if your goal is a compact build. While it offers nothing special, it's still a worthy upgrade from the M03. It has 4mm tempered glass on both sides, providing your components with top-notch protection. This case also sports a mesh front panel, removable magnetic dust filters on the top and bottom, and a mesh cover on the rear to help improve airflow and provide easy cleaning.

Just like the M03, the Vetroo M05 also has a single pre-installed fan. However, there's room for seven more 120mm fans, and it can handle a 240mm radiator with no issues. While the radiator size isn't anything special, the case has enough clearance for GPUs and air coolers at 340mm and 165mm, respectively. Thanks to this clearance, you can easily use the Vetroo M05 for mini-ITX builds, as you can fit large, high-end GPUs into the unit.

It has a simple I/O interface with a USB-C port, two USB-A ports, a reset button, and a power button. The inside is spacious, and cable management is great, too. While the lack of more fans makes it less of a deal, it's still a great case, especially if you already have fans or would be okay with purchasing more separately.

SilverStone SG13P PC case
Alternate best pink mini-ITX case

A budget mini-ITX case that comes in pink

The SilverStone SG13P is an affordable mini-ITX PC case that offers a lot of value for your money. You can consider this case for an entry to a mid-range PC build.

Pros
  • Truly small form factor
  • Enough room for two slot gaming GPUs
  • Cheap
Cons
  • Limited airflow
$64 at Amazon

The SilverStone SG13P is another great option to consider if you're leaning towards a mini-ITX PC build, especially if you want a more typical experience. Compared to the Vetroo M05, the SG13P has some trade-offs that make this chassis more suitable for a budget build than the high-end PC you can put inside the M05.

The SG13P is decently optimized for airflow, with vents on the front, side, and top panels. Intake airflow from the front is aided thanks to the front panel being mesh rather than solid. Unfortunately, that's all offset by limited fan and radiator support; you can only install a single 120mm or 140mm fan on the front and a 120mm or 140mm radiator for liquid cooling. The other vents don't have mounting holes for fans, though they are large enough to exhaust air freely.

Clearance for the GPU and CPU air coolers is also pretty limited. Graphics cards can only be 270mm or shorter and can't be thicker than two slots. CPU air cooler support is limited to 61mm, which is a strong incentive to use a 120mm or 140mm liquid cooler. This is all because the SG13P supports a standard ATX-sized PSU, which keeps the cost of building a PC in this chassis lower than other ITX cases.

SilverStone's SG13P is a great option for anyone trying to build an ITX PC on a budget, since cheaper parts generally consume less power and create less heat, making the lack of cooling potential in the SG13P a non-issue. Upgrading a PC designed around this chassis might be challenging, however.

Apevia Genesis Pro-G-PK PC case
Best pink PC case with RGB

You know what goes well with literally every color? Pink.

The Apevia Genesis Pro is a good option for those who like to have lots of RGB lights inside their PC case. This unit comes with six pre-installed RGB case fans.

Pros
  • Six included RGB fans
  • Tempered glass front and side panels
  • Relatively cheap
Cons
  • Front panel restricts airflow
$89 at Amazon

If you're somebody who likes to have an RGB light show inside your PC, then you might want to consider buying the Apevia Genesis G-PRO-PK. It comes with RGB fans and tempered glass panels for both the side and the front, allowing you to appreciate an obscene amount of color for under $100.

Although the Genesis is a bit pricier than some other cases on this list, you're getting what you pay for since this case comes with six preinstalled RGB fans. It's uncommon to find a case with that many fans included, let alone with RGB functionality. You will have to provide your own RGB controller, though.

It's good that the Genesis comes with all these fans because airflow isn't exactly optimal. The front panel is 100% tempered glass, which looks good but leaves only a little gap between the case and the panel for intake airflow. Even though you can install a 360mm radiator on the front and a 240mm on the top, the lack of fresh air will be a challenge for cooling. Thanks to its seven expansion slots, the Genesis supports graphics cards up to 350mm in length and any thickness. The clearance for the CPU air cooler is 160mm, which is enough for pretty much any model.

The Apevia Genesis Pro G-PRO-PK is first and foremost about appearances, even at the expense of practicality. At just under $100, it's actually a really good deal since it comes with six preinstalled RGB fans and glass panels on the front and side. Thermal performance might be a concern if you select high-end parts to pair with this case.

Best pink PC cases to buy: Final Thoughts

Pink cases might not be popular, but there are many good chassis you can get your hands on if you want a pink rig. While the ideal case will depend on the PC you intend to build, the options above are some of the best pink cases you can get. For instance, the Vetroo AL600 case is the best overall due to its relatively low price tag and excellent design.

Vetroo AL600
Best overall pink PC case

The Vetroo AL600 is a mid-tower ATX PC case that comes with a lot of great features for the price. It's available in pink, black, and white color options.

$80 at Amazon $116 at Newegg

At the same time, the Thermaltake AH T200 and Apevia Genesis Pro-G-PK provide top-notch functionality without compromising on looks.

While the above-mentioned brands may not be as popular as NZXT, Corsair, and the like, you're not skimping on quality or functionality. Once you've chosen the ideal case for your rig, you can go through our list of best CPUs and motherboards to determine the components to house in it. Also, remember to check out our XDA Computing Forums, where you can discuss your build, get product recommendations, and more.
item.content:encoded:
Building your PC means customizing it to suit your preferences. However, these days, having a unique build is such a challenging enterprise, thanks to most PC cases sharing similar designs. To overcome this problem and make their rigs unique, most people choose colorful cases instead of the default white and black options.

Pink cases are rather distinct. There aren't many on the market, so you'd be hard-pressed to find many rigs using them. However, we've scoured hundreds of pages to help you find the best pink PC cases to house your GPU, CPU, fans, and other components. While many brands below aren't mainstream manufacturers like NZXT and Corsair, they offer reliable cases with excellent radiator support, a roomy chassis, multiple expansion slots, and more.

    Vetroo AL600
    Best overall pink PC case
    $80 at Amazon

    Alternate best overall pink PC case
    $77 at Amazon
  • Apevia Prodigy-PK micro-ATX gaming case
    Best affordable pink PC case
    $75 at Amazon

    Best pink PC case for airflow
    $125 at Amazon
  • Thermaltake Tower 200
    Best pink liquid cooling PC case
    $130 at Amazon
  • Vetroo M05 micro-ATX computer case
    Best pink micro-ATX case
    $60 at Amazon
  • Source: Silverstone

The top pink PC cases in 2024

Pros
  • Six included RGB fans
  • Mesh front panel for better airflow
  • Tempered glass side panel
Cons
  • Half of the bottom fan is covered up
$80 at Amazon $116 at Newegg

The Vetroo AL600 is a mid-tower ATX gaming PC case available in pink, black, and white color variants. This PC case looks very similar to the Corsair 4000D Airflow PC case but is cheaper. The Vetroo AL600 case comes with a tempered glass side panel that lets you see the internals of the build. It uses a plain panel for the rest of the sides with vents for airflow. The front of the case has a mesh panel to offer maximum airflow and a dust filter to ensure that extra airflow doesn't mean more dust.

One of the best things about this PC case is that it has three pre-installed 120mm ARGB/PWM fans and a controller to control them. Two non-RGB fans are pre-installed on the PSU shroud and another at the back exhaust, bringing the AL600 to six total fans. The AL600 also offers pretty good radiator mounting options: 360mm at the front, 280mm at the top, and 120mm at the back.

The AL600 has seven horizontal expansion slots plus two vertical slots if you want to mount your GPU vertically. A separate compartment at the bottom holds the PSU and HDDs. You can also install more drives thanks to the dedicated mounting space at the back, behind the motherboard. There's enough space inside the chassis to even build a water-cooled PC, complete with a reservoir. Cable management should be pretty easy, thanks to the two wire combs installed behind the motherboard.

Overall, the Vetroo AL600 is a fantastic PC case that comes with all the essentials while only costing about $140. Many mainstream PC cases on the market with similar features cost significantly more than that. Most importantly, the AL600 doesn't exist just for being a pink PC case; it's a good PC case that's available in pink, an important distinction.

Apevia Crusader-F-PK PC case
Alternate best overall pink PC case

item.title: How I filter out all ads on all my devices with my NAS
item.itunes:title: How I filter out all ads on all my devices with my NAS
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
A network-attached storage (NAS) device can be used for various tasks, including blocking website advertisements. If you've grown tired of being tracked online, desire some privacy, and don't want your PC grinding to a halt due to the loading of five automatic videos and six resource-intensive advert blocks, AdGuard may be what you need in your online life. I'm going to show you how to set it up on a Synology NAS enclosure. You can now store all your files, run a media server, manage surveillance, and block ads all on the same device.

What you'll need

To run AdGuard on a Synology NAS device, you'll need a Synology enclosure running the latest release of DiskStation Manager, some storage drives, and Container Manager installed. Just about any Synology NAS will do, including the budget-friendly DS223j with its Arm processors, 1GB of RAM, and two drive bays. So long as the NAS is running DSM and you have some space for an additional app, you can install AdGuard. If you're unfamiliar with the name, AdGuard is a domain name system (DNS), privacy protection, and ad-blocking software, similar to Pi-Hole and alternative solutions.

It's available for Linux, macOS, Windows, Android, and iOS. With AdGuard running on a NAS enclosure, you can protect multiple devices from unwanted ads and other content. How it works is by intercepting all data traffic and filtering it against preset rules. This prevents hardware from connecting to servers that track usage, as well as external sources of advertisements and known phishing and malware. With a Synology NAS enclosure, some drives, and AdGuard at the ready, we can install the software and enjoy a streamlined online experience.

Related
Best NAS devices in 2024
Expand your PC storage with one of these NAS enclosures

How to set up AdGuard on a Synology NAS

Here's how to install AdGuard on a Synology NAS:

  1. Log into your Synology NAS.
  2. Open Package Center.
  3. Search for and install Container Manager.
  4. Open File Manager.
  5. Open the docker shared folder. CHECK
  6. Create a new folder called "adguard."
  7. Open Container Manager.
  8. Go to Registry.
  9. Search for and download adguard/adguardhome.
  10. Choose latest.
  11. Click Apply.
  12. Go to Container.
  13. Click Create.
  14. Choose adguard/adguardhome:latest.
  15. Check "Enable auto-restart."
  16. Click Next.
  17. Enter 3000 into the empty port field for the 3000 container port.
  18. Click Add Folder.
  19. Choose the AdGuard folder we created in step 6.
  20. Enter /data into the new empty field.
  21. Repeat steps 17-19 but enter /config into the field.
  22. Click Next.
  23. Open a new tab and enter YourSynologyNASIP:3000.
  24. Click Get Started.
  25. Enter a new port for the admin web interface, changing it from the default 80.
  26. Click Next.
  27. Enter credentials.
  28. Click Next.
  29. Select each platform and follow the instructions to configure all your network-connected devices. (This can be done later.)
  30. Click Next.
  31. Go back to the browser tab with Container Manager open.
  32. Click adguard/adguardhome.
  33. Click Stop.
  34. Click Settings.
  35. Click Add under Port Settings.
  36. Enter your chosen admin web interface port twice from step 25.
  37. Click Save.
  38. Return to the AdGuard installer tab.
  39. Click Open Dashboard.
  40. Log in with your credentials from step 27.

You can now monitor AdGuard through this dashboard, configure the container, and view instructions for setting up devices. With privacy concerns abound and the threat of malware on the rise, it's important to take steps such as these to protect you and those around you when online.

item.title: How to take a screenshot on a Mac in more than one way
item.itunes:title: How to take a screenshot on a Mac in more than one way
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Taking a screenshot is one of the essential features that many users rely on when using their devices. While this functionality is pretty straightforward on most recent phones, utilizing it on macOS Sonoma can be more tricky. However, worry not, because once you get the hang of it, you likely won't struggle with using it.

In this article, we'll take a look at how to take a basic screenshot, as well as some more advanced options. For those comfortable with Terminal commands, we'll also see how to take a screenshot on Mac using this method. Lastly, we will take a brief look at how to store your screenshots in a custom folder for organizational purposes.

Related
How to transfer files from Android to Mac in two ways
If you're looking for simple ways to transfer files between Android and Mac devices, you're in luck

How to take a screenshot on Mac using keyboard shortcuts

The simplest way to take a screenshot on your Mac is to use the built-in keyboard shortcuts. All of our favorite Macs have this functionality built-in. If you're interested in capturing a full image of the entire screen on your Mac, simply press Shift + Command + 3 (all buttons simultaneously). This will capture everything on your desktop and save the picture to your desktop as a PNG file.

Often though, you may want to just take a screenshot of a particular portion of your screen. To do this, press Shift + Command + 4. This command brings up a crosshair which allows you to choose a portion of your screen with a quick mouse click and slide. After dragging to highlight the desired portion, simply release the mouse pressure and the screenshot of the selected zone will save to your desktop.

There are also other screenshot-related keyboard shortcuts that we've highlighted in our guide to recording the screen on a Mac, including:

  • Capture your working window or menu: Shift + Command + 4 + Spacebar
  • Launch the Screenshot app: Shift + Command + 5

By default, macOS will name your screenshot using the date and time it was taken. If you are interested in changing the name or file format of your screenshot, see the section below covering Terminal commands.

How to take a screenshot on Mac with third-party apps

There are also a number of solid third-party apps that can take and edit screenshots on your Mac. All of these apps offer basic screenshot functionality and a variety of additional advanced tools based on your particular use case. Below are some of our favorite picks if you want to check out third-party options.

  • Lightshot

    Lightshot is a free third-party app that offers a fast and easy way to use interface. After capturing your screenshot, you can easily share to social media or save to your favorite cloud platform.

    See at Apple Apps
  • SnapNDrag

    SnapNDrag is a paid app, with a price of $10, but it's well worth it. The ability to organize, annotate, and share screenshots is seamless. If you need to sync screenshots across devices or social channels, this is the app for you.

    $10 at Apple Apps
  • Monosnap

    Monosnap offers a free version with a robust feature set. Featuring an 8x magnifier window, this is the app for pixel-perfect screenshots. In addition, you can set custom hotkeys and take delayed screenshots. There's a paid version with additional commercial features.

    See at Apple Apps

How to take and rename screenshots on Mac using Terminal

If you're an advanced macOS user, you've likely used Terminal at some point. Terminal commands allow you to control your Mac on a more granular level, and also avoid using the mouse so much. Follow the steps below to capture and rename screenshots.

  1. The Terminal app is located within the Utilities folder on your Mac.
  2. Opening Terminal will display a simple command-line interface to enter text.
  3. To take an interactive screenshot using Terminal, type the following command (customized as you wish), then press Return. 
    screencapture -i ~/Downloads/myScreenCapture1.jpg

This command will bring up the crosshair so that you can select the desired portion of the screen. After selecting the portion you wish to include, Terminal saves the file to your Downloads folder as a JPG file with the name "myScreenCapture1" in this case. The main benefit of using Terminal for screenshots seems clear: it makes it easy to customize the file name, format, and saved location.

It's worth noting that the "-i" extension in the Terminal command above tells your Mac to take an interactive screenshot. This is the portion of the command that enables the crosshair selection tool. There are a number of other extensions you may want to use with this command in Terminal. For instance, adding the -x command will take a screenshot with no animation sound, and -C will force the screenshot to show the cursor. It's also possible to combine several command extensions simultaneously. 

screencapture -ixC ~/Dropbox/myScreenCapture3.png

The command above saves your interactive screenshot to the Dropbox folder, the screenshot animation doesn't make any sound, and your screenshot will include the cursor. With Terminal commands, control over the screenshot function is much more precise, and you can even change the default settings.

How to change the default file format or folder for screenshots using Terminal

After using Terminal to take a few screenshots, you may decide you want to manage your screenshots even more carefully by changing your default preferences. To change the default save location for screenshots use the following command, with the desired folder in place of "Dropbox/Screenshots." 

defaults write com.apple.screencapture location ~/Dropbox/Screenshots​

This is a very convenient option for automatically saving your screenshots to a folder in the cloud, as I've done in this instance. In addition to the default folder, you may also wish to change the default file format for screenshots. The default format can be easily modified to PDF, JPG, TIFF, or GIF format. For example, to change the default screenshot format to JPG, use the following command: 

defaults write com.apple.screencapture type jpg

Replacing the JPG extension with any of the other formats will generate a similar result. As you can see, it's quite simple to take full control of your screenshot output using Terminal on Mac.

Capture a Mac screen like a pro

There are many ways to take a screenshot on a Mac. If you're simply after basic functionality, you're fine sticking with the built-in keyboard shortcuts. For those looking for a dedicated tool for taking a high number of screenshots, many of the third-party apps offer excellent annotation and sharing features for social media and cloud integration. Using the Snagit app, you can even add picture-in-picture to a tutorial using one of our favorite webcams for Mac. Advanced users will love taking control of every aspect of the screenshot experience using Terminal commands. Whichever method you choose to use, you are now fully prepared for any task that requires screenshots on your Mac.

item.title: Save half off this Blink Outdoor 4-pack that provides home protection on the cheap
item.itunes:title: Save half off this Blink Outdoor 4-pack that provides home protection on the cheap
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Home security used to be complicated and expensive. Now, you can pick up multi-cam kits that are easy to set up and can have your home well protected in just a few hours. The Blink Outdoor is one prime example of what you want when you're looking for a security camera, and this 4-pack set it now available for practically a steal.

Related
Best smart locks in 2024
Ditch your house keys and lock your door from anywhere

It's a complete wireless camera system that's also incredibly low maintenance

Blink Outdoor 4 camera system
$170 $340 Save $170

A 4-camera system that offers impressive video quality and easy maintenance, with just two AA batteries powering it for up to two years. Right now, you can score this collection for 50% off for a limited time.

$170 at Amazon

. While this 4-pack camera system typically comes in priced at a hefty $340, you can now score a fantastic discount that drops the price by half, coming in at just $169.99 for a limited time.

What's great about this Blink Outdoor camera system?

Source: Blink

The Blink camera system really delivers when it comes to the experience, with impressive video quality that comes in with a resolution of 1080p, which means that whatever is being filmed will look clear and crisp. And with added perks like active tracking and night vision, you're going to get excellent results no matter the situation.

In addition, the cameras offer two-way communication, which means you'll be able to talk with whoever is on the other side when needed. The cameras are connected, offering instant notifications when something's picked up, with alerts on compatible devices like smartphones and tablets.

We mentioned durability and reliability before, and the Blink Outdoor 4 can really handle it all, with weather sealing so it can handle even the wettest environments, and operating temperatures that can range from -4 to 113° F. And when it comes to maintenance, just two AA batteries can power these cameras for up to two years.

Perhaps best of all, you can save all your videos locally with the included Blink Sync Module 2. Just plug in a storage device, and you can access all your files directly from your own network. If you want additional cloud storage of your clips, you can also subscribe to Blink's optional service for a small fee.

For the money, you're getting the best bang for the buck here, with four cameras that come in at $169.99. And if you want to add more cameras later, that's also a possibility too. So get this deal while you can because this deal won't last long.
